title: At the Man2oushe Shop
setting: A busy neighborhood bakery (furn) in Beirut early in the morning. The smell of fresh za'atar and cheese fills the air. You walk up to the counter to order breakfast.

branches.speaker: Narrator
branches.arabic: الصبح بكير، وصلت عالفرن. في ناس عم يطلبو مناقيش.
branches.transliteration: Iṣ-ṣubiḥ bakkīr, wṣilt ʕal-furn. Fī nās ʕam yuṭlubū manāqīsh.
branches.english: Early morning, you arrive at the bakery. People are ordering manoushe.

branches.speaker: Baker
branches.arabic: أهلا وسهلا! شو بتحب تاخد؟
branches.transliteration: Ahla w sahla! Shū btiḥibb tēkhod?
branches.english: Welcome! What would you like to have?
branches.culturalTip: Lebanese bakeries are called 'furn' (فرن). Manoushe is the most popular breakfast food — a flatbread with toppings baked fresh to order.

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose what to order:

choices.culturalNote: Mixing za'atar and cheese on one manoushe is very common and called 'mnousse' by some.

branches.speaker: Baker
branches.arabic: طيّب! بدّك ياها كبيرة ولّا زغيرة؟
branches.transliteration: Ṭayyeb! Baddak yēha kbīre walla zghīre?
branches.english: Okay! Do you want it big or small?

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose the size:

branches.speaker: Baker
branches.arabic: بدّك شي تاني؟ عنّا لبنة وعنّا عصير ليمون.
branches.transliteration: Baddak shī tēnī? ʕAnna labne w ʕanna ʕaṣīr laymūn.
branches.english: Do you want anything else? We have labneh and we have lemonade.
branches.culturalTip: Labneh (strained yogurt) spread on flatbread is another classic Lebanese breakfast item, often drizzled with olive oil.

branches.speaker: You
branches.arabic: لأ شكرًا، بس هيك.
branches.transliteration: Laʔ shukran, bass hayk.
branches.english: No thank you, just that.

branches.speaker: Baker
branches.arabic: تكرم! دقيقة وبتكون جاهزة.
branches.transliteration: Tikram! Daʔīʔa w btikūn jēhze.
branches.english: Of course! One minute and it'll be ready.
branches.culturalTip: The word 'tikram' (تكرم) means 'you are honored' and is used like 'of course' or 'at your service' in Lebanese Arabic.

branches.speaker: Narrator
branches.arabic: الخبّاز حطّ المنقوشة بالفرن. بعد شوي طلعت جاهزة.
branches.transliteration: Il-khabbāz ḥaṭṭ il-manʔūshe bil-furn. Baʕd shwayy ṭilʕit jēhze.
branches.english: The baker puts the manoushe in the oven. After a bit, it comes out ready.

branches.speaker: Baker
branches.arabic: تفضّل! هيدي إلك. صارو عشرين ألف ليرة.
branches.transliteration: Tfaḍḍal! Hayde ilak. Ṣārū ʕishrīn alf līra.
branches.english: Here you go! This is yours. That's twenty thousand lira.

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose how to respond:

choices.text: Here you go, keep the change
choices.culturalNote: Telling someone to keep the change is a nice gesture. Small tips are appreciated at bakeries.

branches.speaker: Baker
branches.arabic: صحتين! تعا كل يوم عنّا!
branches.transliteration: Ṣaḥtayn! Taʕa kull yōm ʕanna!
branches.english: Bon appetit! Come every day to us!
branches.culturalTip: 'Sahtayn' (صحتين) literally means 'two healths' and is the standard way to wish someone a good meal in Lebanese Arabic.

comprehensionCheck.question: What does 'shū btiḥibb tēkhod?' mean?
options: What would you like to have?

comprehensionCheck.question: What does 'sahtayn' mean when said after ordering food?
How do you say 'small one is enough' in Lebanese Arabic?

title: Taking a Service Taxi
setting: A busy street in Beirut. You need to get to Hamra. You see a shared taxi (service) approaching and wave it down.

branches.speaker: Narrator
branches.arabic: إنت واقف عالطريق بالأشرفية. بدّك تروح عالحمرا. شفت سرفيس جايي.
branches.transliteration: Inta wēʔif ʕaṭ-ṭarīʔ bil-Ashrafiyye. Baddak trūḥ ʕal-Ḥamra. Shift servīs jēyī.
branches.english: You are standing on the road in Ashrafieh. You want to go to Hamra. You see a service taxi coming.
branches.culturalTip: A 'service' (سرفيس) is a shared taxi in Lebanon. You stand on the side of the road, wave one down, and tell the driver your destination through the window. If they're going your way, they nod and you get in.

branches.speaker: You
branches.arabic: سرفيس! عالحمرا؟
branches.transliteration: Servīs! ʕal-Ḥamra?
branches.english: Service! To Hamra?

branches.speaker: Driver
branches.arabic: يلّا اطلع! وين بالحمرا بالزبط؟
branches.transliteration: Yalla iṭlaʕ! Wayn bil-Ḥamra biz-zabṭ?
branches.english: Come on, get in! Where in Hamra exactly?

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose where you're going:

choices.culturalNote: AUB (American University of Beirut) is a famous landmark in Hamra. Using landmarks for directions is very common in Lebanon.

branches.speaker: Driver
branches.arabic: ماشي. بوصّلك. هالطريق فيها شوية زحمة اليوم.
branches.transliteration: Mēshī. Bwaṣṣlak. Haṭ-ṭarīʔ fīha shwayyet zaḥme il-yōm.
branches.english: Fine. I'll get you there. This road has a bit of traffic today.
branches.culturalTip: Traffic (zaḥme) is a constant topic of conversation in Beirut. Commenting on it is a reliable way to make small talk.

branches.speaker: Driver
branches.arabic: إنت من وين؟ مش من هون؟
branches.transliteration: Inta min wayn? Mish min hōn?
branches.english: Where are you from? Not from here?

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose how to answer:

choices.textArabic: أنا من أميركا، عم بتعلّم عربي
choices.textTransliteration: Ana min Amīrka, ʕam bitʕallam ʕarabī

choices.text: My family is Lebanese but I grew up abroad
choices.textArabic: أهلي لبنانيّة بس أنا تربيت برّا
choices.textTransliteration: Ahlī libnēniyye bass ana trabbēt barra
choices.culturalNote: Many Lebanese people live abroad. Taxi drivers love hearing that someone is reconnecting with their roots.

branches.speaker: Driver
branches.arabic: أهلا وسهلا فيك بلبنان! حلو إنّك عم تتعلّم.
branches.transliteration: Ahla w sahla fīk b-Lubnān! Ḥilw innak ʕam titʕallam.
branches.english: Welcome to Lebanon! Nice that you're learning.

branches.speaker: Narrator
branches.arabic: وصلتو عالحمرا. السرفيس وقف.
branches.transliteration: Wṣaltū ʕal-Ḥamra. Is-servīs waʔaf.
branches.english: You arrive in Hamra. The service stops.

branches.speaker: You
branches.arabic: هون منيح. قدّيش؟
branches.transliteration: Hōn mnīḥ. Addaysh?
branches.english: Here is good. How much?

branches.speaker: Driver
branches.arabic: مية ألف ليرة.
branches.transliteration: Miyye alf līra.
branches.english: One hundred thousand lira.
branches.culturalTip: Service taxi prices are generally known by locals. It is okay to ask 'addaysh?' (how much?) if you are unsure.

branches.speaker: You
branches.arabic: تفضّل. يسلمو، يعطيك العافية!
branches.transliteration: Tfaḍḍal. Yislamū, yaʕṭīk il-ʕēfye!
branches.english: Here you go. Thanks, may God give you strength!
branches.culturalTip: 'Yaʕtīk il-ʕēfye' (يعطيك العافية) literally means 'may God give you wellness/strength.' It is commonly said to anyone who is working, as a sign of respect.

comprehensionCheck.question: How do you hail a service taxi in Lebanon?
options: You call a number and book one
You stand on the road, wave it down, and say your destination
You go to a taxi station and wait in line
You use an app on your phone

comprehensionCheck.question: What does 'yaʕṭīk il-ʕēfye' express?
options: May God give you strength (said to a working person)

title: At the Pharmacy
setting: You're not feeling well and walk into a pharmacy (saydaliyye) in your neighborhood. The pharmacist greets you from behind the counter.

branches.speaker: Narrator
branches.arabic: رأسك عم يوجعك ومعدتك مش منيحة. فتت عالصيدلية.
branches.transliteration: Rāsak ʕam yūjaʕak w maʕdtak mish mnīḥa. Fitt ʕaṣ-ṣaydaliyye.
branches.english: Your head hurts and your stomach is not good. You walk into the pharmacy.

branches.speaker: Pharmacist
branches.arabic: أهلين! شو في؟ كيف فيني ساعدك؟
branches.transliteration: Ahlēn! Shū fī? Kīf fīnī sēʕdak?
branches.english: Hello! What's going on? How can I help you?
branches.culturalTip: In Lebanon, pharmacists often act as a first point of medical advice. Many common medicines are available without a prescription.

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose how to describe your problem:

choices.text: My stomach hurts and I feel nauseous
choices.textTransliteration: Maʕdtī ʕam tūjaʕnī w ḥēsis b-ghasyēn

branches.speaker: Pharmacist
branches.arabic: من إمتا عم توجعك؟
branches.transliteration: Min imta ʕam tūjaʕak?
branches.english: Since when has it been hurting?

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose your answer:

branches.speaker: Pharmacist
branches.arabic: عندك حرارة؟
branches.transliteration: ʕAndak ḥarāra?
branches.english: Do you have a fever?

branches.speaker: You
branches.arabic: ما بعرف، بس حاسس حالي تعبان.
branches.transliteration: Ma baʕrif, bass ḥēsis ḥālī taʕbān.
branches.english: I don't know, but I feel tired.

branches.speaker: Pharmacist
branches.arabic: خود هالحبوب. وحدة الصبح ووحدة بالليل بعد الأكل. وإشرب مي كتير.
branches.transliteration: Khūd hal-ḥbūb. Waḥde iṣ-ṣubiḥ w waḥde bil-layl baʕd il-akl. W ishrab mayy ktīr.
branches.english: Take these pills. One in the morning and one at night after eating. And drink lots of water.
branches.culturalTip: Lebanese pharmacists will usually give you specific instructions on how to take medicine, often quite directly and in detail.

branches.speaker: You
branches.english: Choose how to respond:

branches.speaker: Pharmacist
branches.arabic: هيدي كافيين. جرّبن أوّل، وإذا ما تحسّنت، روح عالدكتور.
branches.transliteration: Hayde kēfyīn. Jarribon awwal, w iza ma tḥassanit, rūḥ ʕad-daktūr.
branches.english: These are sufficient. Try them first, and if you don't improve, go to the doctor.

branches.speaker: Pharmacist
branches.arabic: صارو تلتمية ألف ليرة.
branches.transliteration: Ṣārū tlatmiyye alf līra.
branches.english: That's three hundred thousand lira.

branches.speaker: You
branches.arabic: تفضّل. يسلمو كتير!
branches.transliteration: Tfaḍḍal. Yislamū ktīr!
branches.english: Here you go. Thank you very much!

branches.speaker: Pharmacist
branches.arabic: سلامتك! إن شاء الله بتتحسّن بسرعة.
branches.transliteration: Salēmtak! In shāʔ Allāh btitḥassan b-surʕa.
branches.english: Get well! God willing, you'll improve quickly.
branches.culturalTip: 'Salēmtak' (سلامتك) is said to someone who is sick or hurt. It means 'your safety' and expresses concern and well wishes.

comprehensionCheck.question: What does 'shū fī?' mean in this context?
options: What do you want to buy?
What's going on? / What's the matter?

comprehensionCheck.question: How did the pharmacist say to take the pills?
options: One in the morning and one at night after eating
Three times a day with food

comprehensionCheck.question: How do you say 'since yesterday' in Lebanese Arabic?
